key_insight: Mechanoluminescence is governed by two emission pathways—a piezoelectric effect and a universal defect-driven structural reorganization—whose activation depends on whether stress is hydrostatic or shear and on whether the active phase is 1D or 3D.
engineering_relevance: By matching crystal dimensionality, stress mode, and stress-sensitive point defects, engineers can rationally tune light-output strength and response behavior in mechanoluminescent materials.
method: DFT calculations optimized for quantitative point-defect treatment
Systematic comparison of hydrostatic versus shear stress
Modeling two representative systems: SrAl2O4:Eu2+,Dy3+ and Ba4Si6O16:Eu2+,Ho3+
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a systematic computational study that identifies two distinct mechanisms across representative 1D and 3D model materials, rather than relying on a single empirical dataset.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on DFT models of selected representative compounds, so real-material performance may still depend on defect concentrations, microstructure, and synthesis conditions.
paper_title: Deciphering Mechanoluminescence: How the Nature of Mechanical Stress and Structural Dimensionality Shape Mechanisms and Responses
